HTML5 Essentials.aiRubric: Check theoretical understanding of HTML5 Essentials.

lessons.title: HTML Document Structure
lessons.theory:
## The Building Blocks of the Web HTML defines the structure of a webpage. Let's look at a simple example: ```html

Hello World

This is a paragraph.

``` **What this code does:** - `

`: This is an 'opening tag' for a Heading 1. It tells the browser to make the text large and bold, like a title. - `Hello World`: This is the actual text content that the user will see on the screen. - `

`: This is the 'closing tag' (noted by the forward slash `/`). It tells the browser that the heading ends here. - `

...

`: This creates a standard paragraph of text.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Profile Page
1. Create a complete HTML structure
2. Set the title to 'My Profile'
3. Add an h1 with your name, a p with your bio, and an unordered list of 3 skills
lessons.starterCode:
___

___

  • ___
  • ___
  • ___
lessons.solution:
Mabel Chinasa

I am a full-stack developer.

  • Python
  • React
  • FastAPI
lessons.hint: DOCTYPE html, title in head, h1/p/ul in body.
lessons.rubric: Valid HTML5 structure with all required elements.

lessons.title: Links & Images
lessons.theory:
## Opening and Closing Tags An HTML element is defined by a start tag, some content, and an end tag. For example: `

My paragraph

`. The `

` is the start tag, and `

` is the end tag. Some elements, like ``, are empty elements and do not have an end tag.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Gallery Page
1. Create 3 links to your favorite websites
2. Add an image tag (use any URL)
3. Make the image a clickable link
lessons.solution: Google GitHub
lessons.hint: Wrap inside to make it clickable.
lessons.rubric: 3 links present. Image is clickable. Alt text provided.

lessons.title: Forms & Inputs
lessons.theory:
## The HTML Skeleton Every webpage follows a standard structure: ```html My Page

Welcome!

``` **What this code does:** - ``: Tells the browser 'This is a modern HTML5 document'. - ``: The root container for the entire page. - ``: The invisible part of the page. It contains metadata and settings. - ``: Sets the name of the tab in your browser (e.g., 'My Page'). - `<body>`: The visible part of the page. Everything the user actually sees (like the `<h1>`) goes inside here.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Registration Form
1. Create a form with fields: Full Name, Email, Password, Age
2. Use appropriate input types (text, email, password, number)
3. Add a submit button
lessons.starterCode:
<form>
  <label>Name:</label>
  <input type='___' name='name' required>
  
  <label>Email:</label>
  <input type='___' name='email' required>
  
  <label>Password:</label>
  <input type='___' name='password' required>
  
  <label>Age:</label>
  <input type='___' name='age' min='1' max='120'>
  
  <button type='___'>Register</button>
</form>
lessons.solution:
<form>
  <label>Name:</label>
  <input type='text' name='name' required>
  
  <label>Email:</label>
  <input type='email' name='email' required>
  
  <label>Password:</label>
  <input type='password' name='password' required>
  
  <label>Age:</label>
  <input type='number' name='age' min='1' max='120'>
  
  <button type='submit'>Register</button>
</form>
lessons.hint: text, email, password, number are the input types. submit for the button.
lessons.rubric: 4 inputs with correct types. Submit button present.

CSS Styling.aiRubric: Check theoretical understanding of CSS Styling.

lessons.title: Selectors & Properties
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Style a Card
1. Create a `.card` class with padding, background, border-radius, and box-shadow
2. Style the card's h2 with a custom color
3. Style a `.btn` class for a button
lessons.hint: Use rgba for shadow opacity. hex codes for colors.
lessons.rubric: Card has shadow, rounded corners. Button is styled.

lessons.title: Flexbox Layout
lessons.theory:
## Targeting Elements
To style an element, CSS needs to 'select' it first. 
- **Element Selector**: Selects all elements of a type (e.g., `p { color: red; }`).
- **Class Selector**: Selects elements with a specific class, starting with a dot (e.g., `.my-class { color: blue; }`).
- **ID Selector**: Selects a unique element with a specific ID, starting with a hash (e.g., `#my-id { color: green; }`).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Navigation Bar
1. Create a flex container for a navbar
2. Logo on the left, links on the right
3. Center items vertically
4. Add spacing between links
lessons.hint: space-between pushes logo left and links right. center for vertical alignment.
lessons.rubric: Navbar is flex with space-between. Links are flex with gap.

lessons.title: CSS Grid
lessons.theory:
## Everything is a Box
In CSS, every HTML element is wrapped in a box. The Box Model consists of:
- **Content**: The text or image itself.
- **Padding**: Transparent space *inside* the border, pushing content inward.
- **Border**: A line going around the padding and content.
- **Margin**: Transparent space *outside* the border, pushing other elements away.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Photo Gallery Grid
1. Create a 3-column grid with 20px gap
2. Make it responsive (1 column on mobile)
3. Style each grid item as a card
lessons.hint: repeat(3, 1fr) for 3 equal columns. 1fr for single column on mobile.
lessons.rubric: 3-column grid. Responsive to 1 column. Items styled as cards.

JS Basics.aiRubric: Check theoretical understanding of JS Basics.

lessons.title: Variables & Console
lessons.theory:
## Making the Web Interactive
JavaScript (JS) is a programming language that allows you to implement complex features on web pages. While HTML provides structure and CSS provides style, JavaScript makes pages dynamic (e.g., updating content, animating images, validating forms, communicating with servers).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Variable Practice
1. Declare `name` (const), `age` (let), `isStudent` (const)
2. Log a greeting using template literals
3. Change age to age + 1 and log again
lessons.hint: const for fixed values, let for changeable. Template literals use backticks.
lessons.rubric: Variables declared correctly. Template literals used. Age incremented.

lessons.title: Functions & Arrow Functions
lessons.theory:
## Storing Information
Variables act as containers for storing data. In modern JavaScript, we use:
- `const`: For values that will NEVER change (constants).
- `let`: For values that can change later on.
(Avoid using the older `var` keyword).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Calculator Functions
1. Create an arrow function `add(a, b)`
2. Create an arrow function `subtract(a, b)`
3. Create a `calculate(a, b, operation)` that calls the right function
4. Test with a few examples
lessons.hint: Pass the function itself (without parentheses) as an argument.
lessons.rubric: Arrow functions defined. Higher-order function works. Output: 13, 7.

lessons.title: Arrays & Methods
lessons.theory:
## Reusable Blocks of Code
A function is a block of JavaScript code designed to perform a particular task.

```javascript
function addNumbers(a, b) {
  let sum = a + b;
  return sum;
}
```

**What this code does:**
- `function`: The keyword that tells JavaScript we are creating a new function.
- `addNumbers`: The name we decided to give our function so we can call it later.
- `(a, b)`: These are **parameters** (inputs). When we use this function, we will pass it two numbers, and it will internally refer to them as `a` and `b`.
- `let sum = a + b;`: We create a new variable named `sum` and store the result of adding `a` and `b` together.
- `return sum;`: This is the output. The function spits this value back out to whoever called it.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Student Data Processing
1. Given an array of scores, use `.map()` to add 5 bonus points to each
2. Use `.filter()` to keep only passing scores (>= 60)
3. Use `.reduce()` to find the total of passing scores
lessons.hint: .map() transforms, .filter() keeps matches, .reduce() accumulates with initial value 0.
lessons.rubric: All 3 array methods used correctly. Chain produces correct results.

React Fundamentals.aiRubric: Check theoretical understanding of React Fundamentals.

lessons.title: What is React?
lessons.theory:
## Component-Based UI React breaks UIs into small, reusable pieces called components. ```jsx function Welcome(props) { return

Hello, {props.name}

; } ``` **What this code does:** - `function Welcome(props)`: We define a standard JavaScript function. In React, a function that starts with a capital letter is treated as a UI Component. `props` (short for properties) are the inputs passed into this component. - `return (...)`: A component must return the UI it wants to draw on the screen. - `

...

`: This looks like HTML, but it's actually **JSX**. JSX allows us to write HTML-like syntax directly inside JavaScript. - `{props.name}`: The curly braces let us 'escape' back into JavaScript. It takes the `name` variable from the `props` object and injects it directly into the HTML output (e.g., resulting in 'Hello, Alice').
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Create a React Component
1. Define a `ProfileCard` functional component
2. It should accept `props.username` and `props.bio`
3. Return a simulated JSX string representing the UI
lessons.hint: Access props properties using props.propertyName.
lessons.rubric: ProfileCard generates correct simulated HTML strings using props.

lessons.title: State (useState)
lessons.instructions:
## Task: State Simulator
1. Create a `useState` mock function that returns a value and a setter
2. Use it to simulate a counter component's logic
3. Call the setter and see the value update
lessons.hint: Set internalState to newValue. Return [internalState, setState] array.
lessons.rubric: State updates to 1. Re-render preserves state value (1, not 0).

State Management.aiRubric: Check theoretical understanding of State Management.

lessons.title: Global State
lessons.theory:
## The Problem with Props
In React, data is passed from parent components down to child components using 'props'. But what if a deeply nested child needs data from the very top? Passing data down through 10 layers of components that don't need it is called **prop drilling**.
**Global State** (using tools like Redux, Zustand, or React Context) solves this by storing data outside the component tree. Any component can 'subscribe' to the global store and access the data directly.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Simple Global Store
1. Create a Store class that holds state and listeners
2. When state updates, call all listeners
3. This simulates how Redux/Zustand works under the hood
lessons.hint: push() to add listener. listener(this.state) to call it.
lessons.rubric: Listeners are notified on every state change.

lessons.title: Modern Layouts
lessons.theory:
## Modern Layouts
- **CSS Flexbox:** Designed for 1-dimensional layouts (either a single row OR a single column). It is fantastic for aligning items, distributing space, and centering content.
- **CSS Grid:** Designed for 2-dimensional layouts (rows AND columns simultaneously, like a checkerboard or a photo gallery).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Flexbox Align
1. You have a simulated container with elements.
2. Match the flexbox property to what it does.
lessons.hint: space-between, center, column.
lessons.rubric: Properties correctly matched.

lessons.title: Intro to Next.js
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Routing in Next.js
1. Next.js uses file-based routing. Match the file path to its URL route.
2. Match: `pages/index.js`, `pages/about.js`, `pages/blog/[id].js`
3. URL patterns: `/`, `/about`, `/blog/123`
lessons.hint: index.js maps to the root (/). Brackets [id] mean a dynamic route.
lessons.rubric: Files correctly mapped to their Next.js URLs.

Frontend Performance.aiRubric: Check theoretical understanding of Frontend Performance.

lessons.title: Lazy Loading
lessons.theory:
## Don't Load Everything at Once
If a user visits your homepage, they shouldn't have to download the JavaScript for your 'Account Settings' page, nor should they download the heavy high-resolution images at the very bottom of the page before they scroll down.
- **Code Splitting**: Breaking your massive JS bundle into smaller chunks that load only when needed.
- **Lazy Loading**: Only loading images or components when they actually enter the viewport (the visible part of the screen).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Lazy Image Simulator
1. Simulate a lazy loading image script.
2. You have a list of images with a boolean `in_viewport`.
3. If `in_viewport` is True, load the image.
lessons.hint: Check if img['in_viewport'] == True.
lessons.rubric: Successfully downloads hero.jpg and skips the rest.

Web Accessibility.aiRubric: Check theoretical understanding of Web Accessibility.

lessons.title: Semantic HTML
lessons.theory:
## Designing for Everyone
Accessibility (a11y) ensures users with disabilities can use your app. Users who are blind or visually impaired use software called **Screen Readers** to navigate the web. Screen readers rely heavily on Semantic HTML.
If you build a button using a generic `<div>` tag, the screen reader won't know it's clickable. If you use a `<button>` tag, the screen reader announces it as a button and allows the user to click it with their keyboard.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Semantic Refactor
1. You are given a string of bad HTML using only `div` tags.
2. Create a mapped version that replaces the generic tags with semantic ones: `header`, `nav`, `main`, `footer`.
lessons.hint: Match the class name to the semantic HTML5 tag.
lessons.rubric: All 4 generic divs matched to their proper HTML5 semantic tags.

Tailwind CSS Basics.aiRubric: Check theoretical understanding of Tailwind CSS.

lessons.title: Utility-First CSS
lessons.theory:
## Utility-First CSS
Tailwind is a 'utility-first' CSS framework. Instead of writing custom CSS classes like `.my-card` in a separate file, you build your design directly in your HTML using tiny, single-purpose classes.
For example, instead of writing `color: blue; text-align: center;`, you just add the classes `text-blue-500 text-center` directly to the HTML tag.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Apply Tailwind Classes
Style a button to have a blue background, white text, padding, and rounded corners.
lessons.starterCode: <button class="___ ___ px-4 py-2 ___">Click Me</button>
lessons.solution: <button class="bg-blue-500 text-white px-4 py-2 rounded">Click Me</button>
lessons.hint: Use bg-blue-500, text-white, and rounded
lessons.rubric: Correctly applies standard Tailwind classes.

lessons.title: Responsive Design
lessons.theory:
## Deciphering Tailwind
Tailwind classes follow a logical naming convention:
- `bg-red-500`: Background color red, medium shade (500).
- `text-white`: Text color white.
- `p-4`: Padding size 4 on all sides.
- `mt-2`: Margin-Top size 2.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Responsive Grid
Create a grid that is 1 column on mobile, and 3 columns on medium screens.
lessons.starterCode:
Item 1
Item 2
Item 3
lessons.solution:
Item 1
Item 2
Item 3
lessons.hint: Use md:grid-cols-3
lessons.rubric: Applies md:grid-cols-3 correctly.

lessons.title: Declarative Rendering
lessons.theory:
## The Template Syntax
Unlike React which uses JSX, Vue uses an HTML-based template syntax. You write standard HTML and use Vue's special syntax to bind data to the template. When the data changes, Vue automatically updates the HTML.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Interpolation
Use Vue's mustache syntax to render the `message` variable.
lessons.hint: Use {{ message }}
lessons.rubric: Correctly renders the ref using double curly braces.

lessons.title: Typing Props
lessons.theory:
## Catching Errors Early
JavaScript is a dynamically typed language, meaning you can pass a string into a function that expects a number, and it won't crash until the code runs in the browser. 
TypeScript adds 'static typing'. You define the exact 'shape' of your data (e.g., this component requires a `title` which is a string, and an `id` which is a number). If you pass the wrong type, TypeScript throws an error *while you are writing the code*, before you even run it.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Define Button Props
Create an interface for a Button component that requires a string `label` and an optional boolean `disabled`.
lessons.hint: Use label: string and disabled?: boolean
lessons.rubric: Correctly types the required string and optional boolean.

lessons.title: CSS Keyframes
lessons.theory:
## Choosing the Right Tool
- **CSS Animations (Transitions & Keyframes):** Best for simple UI interactions like hovering over a button, simple fading, or loading spinners. They are highly performant because the browser optimizes them.
- **JavaScript Animations (Framer Motion, GSAP):** Best for complex, sequenced animations, dragging elements, or animations that depend on user scroll position or math calculations.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Define a Fade-In
Write a keyframe animation that fades opacity from 0 to 1.
lessons.hint: Use 0 and 1 for opacity.
lessons.rubric: Correctly defines the start and end opacity.

lessons.title: React Testing Library
lessons.theory:
## Ensuring Quality
- **Unit Tests:** Testing individual, isolated pieces of code (e.g., does this one math function work?).
- **Component Tests:** Testing a specific UI component (e.g., does this button render and trigger a click event?).
- **End-to-End (E2E) Tests:** Simulating a real user driving the browser (e.g., Cypress or Playwright). The automated script opens the page, types in the login form, clicks submit, and verifies the dashboard loads.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Assert Element Presence
Write an assertion that checks if an element with the text 'Submit' is in the document.
lessons.hint: Remove the blank space, it just calls toBeInTheDocument()
lessons.rubric: Correctly invokes toBeInTheDocument assertion.

lessons.title: useState Basics
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Initialize State
Use the `useState` hook to initialize a `count` variable to 0.
lessons.hint: Use setCount and useState
lessons.rubric: Correctly sets setCount and useState(0).
